coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as the fluorescence probe for dopamine based on the redox reaction between Cr(VI) and dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Tian
He, Yu
Ge, Yili
Song, Gongwu
coredata.dc:description: Doping is recognized as a powerful way to tailor the defect in the surface and modify the properties of Carbon-based (C-based) materials. Various state-of-the-art techniques have been proposed to prepare boron and nitrogen co-doped C-based materials. Here we first introduced 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) as the single starting material to fabricate boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (N/B-CDs) with high quantum yield (25.9%) through one-pot hydrothermal method without further passivation. We applied the as-prepared N/B-CDs as a probe to detect Cr(VI) based on the inner filter effect (IFE) and dopamine based on the redox reaction, and the linear ranges were 0.25–243μM for detection of Cr(VI) and 1.4–260μM for detection of dopamine (DA), respectively. Moreover, this probe was utilized to analysis of dopamine in serum and urine samples, which was presaged more opportunities for application in biological field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence
Cr(VI)
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The sensitivity of N/B-CDs as the fluorescence sensor for Cr(VI) and DA
The feasibility of N/B-CDs as the fluorescence sensor for Cr(VI) and DA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesis of N/B-CDs and sensing mechanism of Cr(VI) and DA.

a) TEM image of N/B-CDs. Inset is the corresponding particles size distribution. b) HRTEM image of N/B-CDs. c) XRD spectrum of N/B-CDs. d) UV–vis absorption spectra of N/B-CDs and APBA. e) FT-IR spectra of N/B-CDs and APBA. f) Emission spectra of N/B-CDs at different excitation wavelength from 340nm to 520nm.
a) Entire XPS scanning spectrum of N/B-CDs. XPS high-resolution survey scan of C 1s b), N 1s c) and B 1s d) of the N/B-CDs.

a) Fluorescence responses of N/B-CDs with different concentration of Cr(VI). Inset: the relationship between F0-F and concentration of Cr(VI). b) The linear relationship plot between F0-F and concentration.

a) Kinetic performance of N/B-CDs-Cr(VI) system with DA; b) Fluorescence responses of N/B-CDs-Cr(VI) with different concentration of DA; c) The relationship between F0-F and concentration of DA; d–f) The linear relationship plot between F0-F and concentration in the range from 0.25 to 243μM (d), 0.25 to 4.6μM (e), and 4.6 to 243μM (f).
Selectivity of the N/B-CDs-Cr(VI) to different molecules with the concentration of 50μM.

a) The UV–vis spectra of N/B-CDs, N/B-CDs+Cr(VI)+DA and Cr(VI); b) The fluorescence intensity of (a) 10μLN/B-CDs, (b) 10μLN/B-CDs+100μM DA, (c) 10μLN/B-CDs+450μM Cr(VI) and (d) 10μLN/B-CDs+450μM Cr(VI)+ 100μM DA.
Analytical results for actual biological fluid.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as the fluorescence probe for dopamine based on the redox reaction between Cr(VI) and dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan, 430062, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doping is recognized as a powerful way to tailor the defect in the surface and modify the properties of Carbon-based (C-based) materials. Various state-of-the-art techniques have been proposed to prepare boron and nitrogen co-doped C-based materials. Here we first introduced 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) as the single starting material to fabricate boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (N/B-CDs) with high quantum yield (25.9%) through one-pot hydrothermal method without further passivation. We applied the as-prepared N/B-CDs as a probe to detect Cr(VI) based on the inner filter effect (IFE) and dopamine based on the redox reaction, and the linear ranges were 0.25–243μM for detection of Cr(VI) and 1.4–260μM for detection of dopamine (DA), respectively. Moreover, this probe was utilized to analysis of dopamine in serum and urine samples, which was presaged more opportunities for application in biological field.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Doping is recognized as a powerful way to tailor the defect in the surface and modify the properties of carbon-based materials (C-based materials) [1–4]. Boron (B), nitrogen (N), sulfur (S) and phosphorus (P) are the most popular heteroatoms for doping in C-based materials. B and N, the neighboring elements of carbon (C) in the periodic table, have atomic radii similar to C, which make it possible to efficiently modulate the properties of C-based materials after doping. Many state-of-the-art techniques have been proposed to prepare B and N co-doped C-based materials [5–8]. Carbon dots (CDs), one of the C-based materials, are superior to quantum dots and metal nanoclusters in terms of excellent physicochemical and photochemical properties, good biocompatibility, low toxicity and easy to preparation [9–13]. However, the desire for novel and simple methods to fabricate a novel B and N co-doped CDs is still challenging. Most of previous methods introduce two or more compounds as the starting materials to synthesize B and N co-doped CDs. Single starting material to synthesize B and N co-doped CDs, containing B and N simultaneously, can simplify the synthesis process and reduce the byproducts.

Dopamine (DA), a kind of catecholamine neurotransmitter which helps cell transmitting pulse, is produced by decarboxylation of 3,4-dihydroxy phenylalanine [14]. DA can treat depression and be conducive to memory boosting, which contribute to the treatment of Alzheimer’s disease. Moreover, DA has certain effects on cure of myocardial infraction, hypertension, bronchial asthma and acute heart surgery [15]. The abnormal concentration of DA may give rise to patient’s hands and feet tremors involuntary or cause Parkinson’s disease [16–18]. Due to the physiological and pathophysiological effects of DA, it is essential to develop a quantitive determination method for DA. In the past decade, various techniques have been proposed to develop DA detection, including high performance liquid chromatography [19], electrochemical methods [20] and enzymatic methods [21]. These methods are generally hard to overcome the defeats of time-consuming, complicated operation and high cost. Fluorescence methods have attracted more interest because of their advantages such as rapid and simple determination, cheaper cost, and maneuverability in biological matrix [22].

Several studies have investigated the redox properties of catechols and DA derivatives with transition metal ions [23–25]. The [Cr(quinone)3]3+ to [Cr(catecholate)3]3− series of complexes have been obtained both via direct synthesis from Cr(III) and substituted catechols and by reduction of Cr(VI) with catechols [26–29]. Therefore, developing a method to detect DA through redox reaction between DA and Cr(VI) is feasible. Cr(VI) is a major carcinogen, and it is equally important to propose a sensor for sensing Cr(VI).

In this work, we first introduced 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) as starting material to fabricate B and N co-doped carbon dots (N/B-CDs) with high quantum yield (25.9%) through one-pot hydrothermal method without further passivation. We designed Cr(VI) as the quencher to quench the fluorescence of N/B-CDs through inner filter effect (IFE) [30]. Then, the fluorescence of N/B-CDs was further quencher by DA through the redox reaction between DA and Cr(VI). Moreover, this probe was utilized to analysis of dopamine in serum and urine samples, which was presaged more opportunities for application in biological field.

APBA, dopamine hydrochloride, glucose (Glu), urea were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ascorbic acid (AA), cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH), tryptophan (Try) and glycine (Gly)were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) and citric acid (CA) were purchased from Tianjing Taixing Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China) and Bodi Chemical Holding Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China) respectively. Human serum samples were collected from healthy volunteers at the hospital, and urine samples were collected from healthy humans in our laboratory. All solutions were freshly prepared before use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a Tecnai G20 microscope (FEI, America). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were performed on JEM-2100UHR STEM/EDS (JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by a D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker, Germany. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed with a VGEscalab 200 spectrometer using an aluminum amode (AlKα) opertaing at 510W with a background pressure of 2×10−9 mbar. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on FT-IR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, America). UV–vis absorption spectra (UV–vis) were performed on Lamber 35 UV spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, America). The fluorescence measurements were recorded on LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, America). Particles size distribution was recorded on ZS90 Dynamic Light Scattering (MALVERN, UK).

The N/B-CDs were synthesized by a modified hydrothermal methods [31]. In a typically experimental procedure, 0.1g of APBA was dissolved in 10mL ultrapure water, followed by adding 0.2mL of 0.1M NaOH under stirring. Then, the clear and homogeneous solution was transferred into the Teflon-lined autoclave chamber and heated to 180°C for 4h. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min to remove large precipitates. The final purified N/B-CDs was stored at 4°C for further use.

The quantum yield of N/B-CDs was calculated by a slope method with Rhodamine B in ethanol solution as a reference. The quantum yield was calculated with the following equation:
Φx
= Φst (Κx /Κst ) (ηstηx )2
where Φ is quantum yield, Κ is the slope of the curve, η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “st” refers to the referenced fluorophore (Rhodamine B in ethanol) with known quantum yield and “x” refers as the samples (CDs and Ag-CD nanohybrid in this work) for the determination of quantum yield.

For Cr(VI) assay, amounts of 150μL 0.1M PBS buffer solution (pH=4) and 5μL purified N/B-CDs were placed into a series of 5mL colorimetric tubes. Subsequently, different amounts of K2Cr2O7 were added, followed by adding ultrapure water to 2mL. After incubation of 1min, fluorescent spectra were recorded on fluorescence spectrometer.

For DA assay, 10μL purified N/B-CDs and 30μL 0.03MK2Cr2O7 were added into 5mL colorimetric tube. Then, different level of DA with different concentration was respectively added into the mixture. After that, the mixture was diluted to 2mL and mixed thoroughly. Eighty minutes later, fluorescent spectra were obtained on fluorescence spectrometer.

The potential application of the proposed bioassay is demonstrated by applying it to detect DA in urine and serum samples. As the results of the experiments, the urine and serum were diluted to avoid the interference from urine and serum sample. No sample pretreatments are used except for an appropriate dilution.

The N/B-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. We selected the APBA as the nitrogen and boron-containing starting material. Under the condition of high temperature and high pressure, the structure of phenylboronic acid would break up to form carbon dots [31]. In order to optimize the photoluminescence performance of N/B-CDs, several parameters were investigated. In a typical experiment optimization, the amount of APBA was kept a constant value, 0.1g. The other synthesis parameters varied as follows: (a) the amount of NaOH (0 to 0.08mM); (b) reaction temperature (120 to 200°C); (c) reaction time (1 to 8h). The optimal conditions for synthesis of N/B-CDs were 0.1g of APBA reacting with 0.02mM of NaOH in the reactor at180°C for 4h, respectively (in Fig. S1).

The morphological structure of N/B-CDs characterized by TEM was displayed at Fig. 1 a. The TEM image demonstrated that the N/B-CDs showed well-dispersed without obvious aggregation with diameter ranged from 1.1 to 3.8nm, and the average size was 2.8nm. By the HRTEM (Fig. 1b), N/B-CDs were observed to have a discernible lattice structure. The lattice fringes on N/B-CDs with an interplanar spacing of 0.214nm could be indexed to the facet of graphitic carbon (100). The XRD pattern (Fig. 1c) of N/B-CDs displayed two peaks at 26° and 42°, which were attributed to graphite lattice spacing (002) and (100). The as-prepared N/B-CDs displayed brown color and yellow emitting under the illumination of 420–500nm. At 230–300nm, both N/B-CDs and their precursor (APBA) showed two strong absorption peaks. As shown in Fig. 1d, the strong peak of 235nm was ascribed to n→π* transition of CCN, and the peak of 290nm was attributed to π→π* transition of CC bond. Moreover, there was an absorption edge extending to 380nm in N/B-CDs, which demonstrated the formation of nanocarbon [32]. The surface composition of the as-prepared N/B-CDs was investigated. In Fig. 1e, the peak at 3375cm−1 in the FT-IR curve of N/B-CDs was ascribed to NH vibrations, corresponding to the peaks at 3470 and 3389cm−1of APBA. The asymmetric stretching vibration of BO at 1357cm−1 shifted to 1372cm−1 in the process of formation of N/B-CDs. Above indicated that B and N had been transferred from APBA to N/B-CDs successfully in the process of materials formation. For a better insight of N/B-CDs, XPS measurements were achieved. In Fig. 2 a, N/B-CDs contained C, O, N and B four elements, and their contents were 56.98%, 21.01%, 7.49% and 14.52%, respectively. B element content was higher than in APBA (8.53%), which implied B element played an important role in the process of formation of N/B-CDs. XPS high-resolution survey scan spectra were shown in Fig. 2b–d. XPS analysis of the C 1s spectrum demonstrated four peaks at 283.9, 284.6, 285.2 and 285.4eV, and they are attributed to CB, CC/CC, CO and CN, respectively. In the high-resolution N 1s spectrum, three fitted peaks at 399.4, 400.2 and 401.5eV could be assigned to CNC, NC and NH, respectively. From the B 1s spectrum, the two peaks at 191.5 and 192.5eV were ascribed to BC and BO, respectively.

In Fig. 1f, the fluorescence spectra indicated that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of N/B-CDs were 490nm and 540nm, respectively. The maximum emission wavelength of N/B-CDs at 540nm remained unchanged with the excitation wavelength increasing from 400nm to 520nm, and the highest fluorescence intensity appeared at excitation wavelength of 490nm (Fig. S2). The excitation-independent photoluminescence behavior might be ascribed to the less surface defects and the narrow particle size distributions of N/B-CDs [33,34]. In addition, the N/B-CDs was observed to be relatively stable emission at broad ranges of pH (2 to14), under continuous irradiation by ultraviolet light, in long-term storage under ambient conditions, and under high ionic strength environments (Fig. S3, S4, S5, S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensitivity of N/B-CDs as the fluorescence sensor for Cr(VI) and DA
ce:section.ce:para: For better performance of sensing of Cr(VI), the experimental conditions were optimized. As shown in Fig. S7a, the quenching efficiency was higher at acidic conditions than basic conditions. And it reached the maximum at pH=4. Fig. S7b showed the reaction time between Cr(VI) and N/B-CDs, the result demonstrated that maximum quenching of fluorescence was observed as Cr(VI) was added into the N/B-CDs matrix 1min later, and no further distinct quenching of fluorescence were observed even after 3min. Under the optimized conditions discussed above, different concentrations of Cr(VI) in the range from 0μM to 3.8mM response was investigated. In Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with the increasing of the concentration of Cr(VI). There was a good linear relationship (R2 =0.997) between fluorescence intensities and concentrations of Cr(VI) in the range from 1.39 to 260μM with a limit detection of 0.28μM (Fig. 3b). The fluorescence intensities versus the concentration of Cr(VI) was fitted into a linear equation: F0-F=0.23[Cr(VI)](μM)+4.2.

To obtain the optimal fluorescence response of the N/B-CDs-Cr(VI) system with DA, the optimized reaction time was investigated. In Fig. 4 a–c, with the addition of DA, the fluorescence intensity of N/B-CDs-Cr(VI) system decreased gradually. After 80min, the fluorescence intensity changed slightly and tended to be stable. Therefore the reaction time was fixed at 80min. As shown in Fig. 4d–f, the fluorescence emissions of N/B-CDs-Cr(VI) at 540nm had different performances with various concentration ranges of DA. In Fig. 4e and f, the difference value between the fluorescence intensity of the system before and after adding DA linearly increased with DA concentration from 0.25 to 4.6μM with a calibration function of F0-F=7.88C+0.81 (C in μM), and from 4.6 to 243μM with a calibration function of F0-F=0.40C+37.33 (C in μM).

Along with the selectivity requirement, high specificity is one of the most vital factor in real sample detection. To confirm whether the proposed sensor was of high specificity for DA detection, we measured the changes in fluorescence intensity in the presence of the representative common molecules including AA, Cys, GSH, Try, Glu, Gly and Urea. F0 and F are fluorescence intensity of the N/B-CDs-Cr(VI) system in the absence and presence of different molecules. In Fig. 5 , only DA had a quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of the N/B-CDs-Cr(VI) system among the tested molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility of N/B-CDs as the fluorescence sensor for Cr(VI) and DA
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of carbon dots quenched by Cr(VI) is based on the IFE [30], where is known to result from the absorption of the exciting and/or emitted light by absorbers in the detection system, which means the Cr(VI) absorbed the exciting and/or light of carbon dots. Cr(VI) exhibited broad absorption at 250, 350, and 440nm, respectively. In the fluorescence spectrum of N/B-CDs, the maximum exciting and emitted wavelength were at 490nm and 530nm, respectively, which didn’t overlap with the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) either. However, those exciting and/or emitted spectra overlap most with absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) have inferior fluorescence response. Therefore, 460nm was chosen as the optimized detection exciting wavelength. In the procedure of N/B-CDs-Cr(VI) reacted with DA, redox reaction happened between Cr(VI) and DA, and the mechanism was shown in Scheme 1 . Adding Cr(VI) into N/B-CDs solution, the IFE happened with the fluorescence weakening. Once DA was added into the N/B-CDs-Cr(VI) system, the redox reaction between Cr(VI) and DA happened. DA was oxidized to dopaquinone, at the same time, the reduction of N/B-CDs-Cr(VI) was involved to generate N/B-CDs-Cr(V) and N/B-CDs-Cr(IV) intermediates and finally N/B-CDs-Cr(III). Immediately after mixing N/B-CDs-Cr(VI) and DA, the solution turned brown and then dark brown during the next 80min. The UV–vis spectra of the solution, shown in Fig. 6 a, exhibited a peak at 370nm which was characteristic of both Cr(V) species and dopaquinone, and the background increased steadily in sight of formation of dopachrome and black polymerized materials (which are probably melanin-like pigments) [26]. In order to verify the response to DA attributed to the introduction of Cr(VI), several control experiments (Fig. 6b) were studied. An ignorable effect on the fluorescence intensity of N/B-CDs was caused in the presence of DA only, exposed that DA couldn’t quench the fluorescence intensity of N/B-CDs without Cr(VI).

In order to investigate the application of the N/B-CDs-Cr(VI) system, the sensor was studied in real biological fluids. The sample was fresh and diluted by 10-fold for further use. The results are shown in Table 1 . The recoveries of DA were in the range of 98.22–100.52%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was lower to 0.5%. The results claimed that the sensor was appropriated to be applied in clinical determination of DA.

In a summary, we have designed nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots using APBA as the single starting material through one-pot hydrothermal methods without further passivation. The as-synthesized N/B-CDs exhibited strong green fluorescence. We applied the as-prepared N/B-CDs as a probe to detected Cr(VI) based on the IFE between N/B-CDs and Cr(VI) and dopamine based on the redox reaction between dopamine and Cr(VI). Moreover, this probe can be utilized to detect dopamine in serum and urine samples. We anticipate that such N/B-CDs will provide broad applications in fluorescence-related areas.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2015CFB273, 2011CDB059 and 2011CDA111) and Wuhan Youth Science and technology plan (2016070204010133).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.09.114.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tian Tian obtained a BS in chemistry education from Hubei University, China. She is currently studying for her MS degree at Hubei University (2014).

Yu He received her PhD in analytical chemistry from Nankai University (2009). After that, she worked as postdoctoral fellow at Nanyang Technological University. In 2010, she joined the Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules. Now she is an associate professor in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Hubei University. Her current interests include analytical chemistry and environmental chemistry.
Yili Ge is an associate professor in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Hubei University. His current interests mainly focus on analytical chemistry.
Gongwu Song is a professor in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Hubei University. His current interests mainly focus on analytical chemistry and environmental chemistry.
